title: Hormuz Closure Shatters Ceasefire Calm as Oil Surges 4-5%, Fed Hawks Circle
subhead: Iran's closure of the Strait of Hormuz and weekend exchange of strikes with the US ended a brief ceasefire-driven market reprieve, sending oil up nearly 5% and the dollar higher. Fed Chair Warsh faces his first congressional testimony with rate hike expectations building, while central banks worldwide grapple with the inflation fallout of a conflict that keeps reigniting.

sections.heading: Strait of Hormuz Closed Again as US-Iran Strikes Resume
sections.body:
Iran declared the Strait of Hormuz closed to shipping over the weekend after the US launched strikes on Iranian military targets, and Iran responded with drone and missile attacks on US bases in Bahrain, Jordan, Kuwait, and Qatar. Oil prices surged roughly 4-5%, with Brent crude advancing $3.34 to $79.50/barrel and WTI gaining $3.07, according to market data cited by multiple outlets including Reuters and Moneycontrol.

The closure effectively nullifies the 60-day ceasefire framework signed just weeks ago between the US and Iran. Iran had previously reopened Hormuz under the interim deal, and spot premiums had fallen back to pre-war levels. That brief normalization is over. Conflicting signals persist: the US military says ships are still transiting via a southern route, while Iran insists the waterway is shut. Maritime insurers are unlikely to normalize coverage until mine-clearing completes — estimated at 40-50 days even in the best case.

Energy firms that had begun planning a return to normal Gulf operations are now re-evaluating. The Reuters Open Interest analysis noted that even before the latest escalation, oil companies were cautioning that a full return to normal for Gulf energy was unlikely despite the ceasefire deal. Iran's demonstrated ability to shut Hormuz at will remains the single largest risk premium in global energy markets.
sections.whyItMatters: The ceasefire's unraveling validates the view that Hormuz risk cannot be priced out while Iran retains the capability and willingness to close the strait. Every cycle of escalation and de-escalation narrows the window for shippers and insurers to operate, gradually making the Gulf a structurally more expensive route.

sections.heading: Fed Chair Warsh's First Big Call: Undo Last Year's Cuts or Hold Steady?
sections.body:
New Federal Reserve Chairman Kevin Warsh will testify before Congress this week in his first appearance since taking the helm, with markets pricing nearly 40 basis points of rate hikes by December, up from about 15 basis points in early June. The federal funds rate currently sits at 3.50%-3.75%, and US inflation ran at 4.2% year-on-year in May — the highest since April 2023, driven largely by energy costs.

According to a detailed analysis by Nick Timiraos in the Wall Street Journal (via LiveMint), some Fed officials who previously championed rate cuts now argue for reversing them. Fed Governor Christopher Waller, who led the case for last year's rate reductions, declared last week that the risks had 'completely flipped.' New York Fed President John Williams noted that semiconductor and electrical equipment prices look like 'hockey sticks' on charts due to AI-driven demand, saying the Fed shouldn't 'look through' sustained demand-side pressure.

But Williams himself counselled patience, saying rates are 'well positioned' and that he's looking for monthly core CPI at 0.2% or lower. The June CPI data, due Tuesday, is the last major input before the Fed's July 28-29 meeting. Warsh has argued against forward guidance, leaving markets to parse the data rather than his words.
sections.whyItMatters: Warsh's testimony is the first real signal of how he intends to run the Fed. The debate is not whether inflation is a problem — it's whether the causes are transitory (tariffs, oil) or structural (AI investment, fiscal expansion). That distinction determines whether the Fed hikes once as a correction or starts a new tightening cycle.

sections.heading: Dollar Surges, Asian Currencies Pressured as Conflict Reignites
sections.body:
The US dollar strengthened against most major currencies on Monday as renewed Middle East conflict fueled inflation fears and rate hike expectations. According to a Reuters report via Dow Jones (cited in Morningstar and SMH), the dollar jumped as investors sought safety and recalculated the inflationary impact of renewed oil supply disruption.

The South Korean won may get an unusual reprieve: SK hynix's $26.5 billion ADR listing — the largest in US history — is expected to channel up to $30 billion in dollar inflows back to Korea, providing a rare tailwind for a currency that recently hit near 17-year lows against the dollar. The Bank of Korea meets Thursday, and all economists in a Bloomberg survey expect a 25 basis point rate hike to 2%, with Governor Shin Hyun Song citing inflation, solid growth, a weak won, and surging home prices.

Earlier in the week, South Korea and Indonesia tightened oversight of speculative currency derivatives trades, as Asian economies heavily reliant on imported energy grapple with the oil-driven currency pressure. The Bank of Japan may revise up its growth forecast for fiscal 2026 while maintaining vigilance on inflation risks from a weak yen and AI demand, three sources told Reuters. The Bank of Canada is expected to hold rates at 2.25% on Wednesday, with a 91% probability priced in by markets.
sections.whyItMatters: The conflict's inflationary spillover is hitting emerging market and trade-dependent economies hardest, forcing central banks in Asia to choose between supporting growth and defending currencies. SK hynix's record listing shows how tech capital flows can temporarily offset geopolitical pressure — but the structural trend of dollar strength on risk-off flows remains.

sections.heading: Trump's 100% Digital Tax Tariff Threat Escalates US-EU Trade Clash
sections.body:
President Trump on June 26 threatened a 100% tariff on all goods from any country that imposes a digital services tax on US companies, explicitly targeting European nations. 'Any Country that imposes such a Tax will immediately be met with a 100% TARIFF on any and all Goods sent to the United States,' Trump wrote, adding that the new tax would supersede any previously negotiated trade deals.

The threat was sharpened at the G7 summit, where Trump specifically targeted French wine and champagne with a 100% tariff unless Paris removes its 3% digital services tax on US tech companies. French President Macron pushed back forcefully, declaring that 'the US does not decide European law' and insisting France would not bow to US pressure on its digital tax.

The EU's spokesperson Olof Gill called unilateral measures 'unjustified' and said the EU would 'respond swiftly and decisively to defend its rights and regulatory autonomy.' The confrontation comes ahead of a July 4 deadline for the EU and US to begin implementing a tariff deal capping most EU exports at 15% — a deal that now looks increasingly fragile. The UK has levied a 2% digital services tax since 2020, and several EU member states are moving toward similar frameworks.
sections.whyItMatters: This is not just about wine. The digital tax fight is the leading edge of a broader US-EU confrontation over who gets to tax the global digital economy. If Trump follows through on a 100% tariff, it would effectively nullify the recently negotiated EU-US trade framework and could trigger retaliatory escalation from Brussels.

sections.heading: Central Banks Accelerate Gold Accumulation, Repatriate Reserves Home
sections.body:
The World Gold Council's 2026 Central Bank Gold Reserves Survey, covering 74 central banks, found that a record 90% of respondents cited gold's crisis performance as a primary reason to hold it. Some 45% plan to increase their own gold reserves over the next 12 months — the highest share since the survey began — while 89% expect global central bank gold reserves to rise overall.

Central banks have purchased an average of 1,000 tonnes of gold annually over the past four years, double the previous decade's average, according to CNBC. The survey also found that 9% of respondents increased domestic gold storage over the past year, up from 5% the year before, while 10% diversified their overseas storage locations, up from just 2%. UBS commodity analyst Giovanni Staunovo told CNBC that Russia's frozen $300 billion in foreign assets 'is driving some central banks to repatriate gold held abroad.'

Tanzania's central bank bought 28 tonnes of gold over the past 18 months to bolster reserves and support the shilling, Reuters reported. Separately, for the first time, more central banks plan to reduce dollar holdings than increase them over the next decade, according to an OMFIF survey cited by MSN.
sections.whyItMatters: The freezing of Russian reserves in 2022 was the catalyst; the Iran conflict is the accelerant. Central banks are not just buying more gold — they're moving it home, where it can't be seized. This is a slow but measurable shift in the architecture of global reserves, driven by the demonstrated willingness of Western governments to weaponize financial infrastructure.

sections.heading: Iranian Oil Piles Up at Sea as China's Teapots Pivot to Rivals
sections.body:
Iranian oil supplies floating at sea are surging as China's independent refiners — known as teapots — turn to alternative Middle East supplies, Reuters reported on July 10. The teapots had been the primary buyers of discounted Iranian crude, but the combination of shipping disruptions through Hormuz and the US-Iran ceasefire's uncertain provisions have complicated those flows.

The US reportedly adopted Iranian-style ship-to-ship transfer tactics to bypass the Hormuz blockade and move crude out of the Gulf during the conflict, according to a separate Reuters report cited in the headlines. But with Hormuz now closed again, even those workarounds face limits. Meanwhile, the ceasefire deal's reported allowance for Iran to sell oil upon signing — and access to a multi-billion-dollar private fund — now looks moot while the strait remains shut.

Asian spot crude premiums had fallen back to pre-war levels after the initial ceasefire deal, but that normalization has been abruptly reversed. The re-closure of Hormuz means refiners across Asia must again scramble for alternative supply, bidding up premiums on non-Gulf grades.
sections.whyItMatters: China's teapots pivoting away from Iranian crude is significant: it shows that even Iran's most reliable customers are finding alternatives when the logistics become unworkable. If this shift hardens, it could permanently reduce Iran's market share in East Asia regardless of whether Hormuz reopens.

sections.heading: G7: Sanctions on Russia Expand, Critical Minerals Plan Faces Skepticism
sections.body:
At the G7 summit in Evian-les-Bains, Canada announced new sanctions targeting 162 individuals, entities, and vessels linked to Russia's shadow fleet and energy sector, following PM Carney's meeting with Zelensky. The UK also unveiled a new sanctions package targeting Russia's shadow oil fleet and finance networks, with British troops seizing a Russian shadow fleet vessel in the English Channel. The EU adopted its own new sanctions package, adding 34 individuals and 47 entities, with a focus on energy revenue flows and propaganda outlets.

Meanwhile, the Trump administration's push for a Western critical minerals pricing bloc faced skepticism from G7 partners and division within the industry, according to headlines. Japan's PM Takaichi proposed a joint critical minerals stockpiling initiative at the summit to counter China's dominance in rare earths, but the broader US plan to regulate prices met resistance from allies concerned about market distortion and from mining companies worried about government price controls.

The G7 also discussed granting select trusted partners access to advanced AI models from US firms like Anthropic, according to three diplomatic sources, though this was overshadowed by the Trump administration's order forcing Anthropic to disable its Fable 5 and Mythos 5 models for security reasons — a move critics called ad hoc regulation.
sections.whyItMatters: The expanding sanctions net on Russia's shadow fleet reflects a coordinated effort to close the financial loopholes that keep Russian oil flowing. But the critical minerals push shows the limits of G7 alignment: allies want to reduce dependence on China, but they disagree on whether government-managed pricing is the right tool.

bottomLine: The weekend's US-Iran escalation shattered the brief market calm that followed the June ceasefire, with Hormuz closed again and oil up 4-5%. The conflict's financial ripple effects are now compounding: inflation expectations are pushing the Fed toward what could be its first rate hike under Warsh, Asian central banks are being forced into tightening, and the dollar is strengthening on safe-haven flows. Meanwhile, the digital tax confrontation between Trump and the EU threatens a separate trade shock entirely unrelated to the Middle East. Central banks are voting with their reserves — buying gold and moving it home — in the clearest signal yet that the current geopolitical environment is reshaping the architecture of global finance, not just the price of oil.
sources: Reuters, Wall Street Journal (via LiveMint), CNBC, World Gold Council, Morningstar/Dow Jones, CryptoBriefing, France24, Bloomberg, SMH (Sydney Morning Herald), News18, Moneycontrol, OMFIF, UBS, Forbes, The Asia Business Daily, Herald Corp (Korea), Yonhap News Agency
